papers.title: An Efficient Gradient-Based Inference Attack for Federated Learning
papers.abstract: Federated Learning is a machine learning setting that reduces direct data exposure, improving the privacy guarantees of machine learning models. Yet, the exchange of model updates between the participants and the aggregator can still leak sensitive information. In this work, we present a new gradient-based membership inference attack for federated learning scenarios that exploits the temporal evolution of last-layer gradients across multiple federated rounds. Our method uses the shadow technique to learn round-wise gradient patterns of the training records, requiring no access to the private dataset, and is designed to consider both semi-honest and malicious adversaries (aggregators or data owners). Beyond membership inference, we also provide a natural extension of the proposed attack to discrete attribute inference by contrasting gradient responses under alternative attribute hypotheses. The proposed attacks are model-agnostic, and therefore applicable to any gradient-based model and can be applied to both classification and regression settings. We evaluate the attack on CIFAR-100 and Purchase100 datasets for membership inference and on Breast Cancer Wisconsin for attribute inference. Our findings reveal strong attack performance and comparable computational and memory overhead in membership inference when compared to another attack from the literature. The obtained results emphasize that multi-round federated learning can increase the vulnerability to inference attacks, that aggregators pose a more substantial threat than data owners, and that attack performance is strongly influenced by the nature of the training dataset, with richer, high-dimensional data leading to stronger leakage than simpler tabular data.

This directly matches the user's problem statement, including the specific mechanism (gradient history in federated learning) and goal (membership inference). | Method: The paper proposes a 'novel gradient-based membership inference attack' that is very similar to existing gradient-based attacks, particularly referencing the work by [13]. The key difference is a focus on 'final-layer gradient data' and using a 'lightweight classification model' to reduce computational cost, rather than computing gradients across all layers as in prior work. This represents an incremental difference in the application of a known method. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on membership inference attacks specifically tailored to federated learning. The paper explicitly states, "we present a new gradient-based membership inference attack for federated learning scenarios that exploits the temporal evolution of last-layer gradients across multiple federated rounds," which directly aligns with the user's idea of exploiting gradient update history from a compromised participant to reconstruct sample usage. | Contribution: The user's research idea proposes a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits gradient update history to reconstruct if a sample was used. This directly matches the user's proposed contribution.

papers.title: Securing Genomic Data Against Inference Attacks in Federated Learning Environments
papers.abstract: Federated Learning (FL) offers a promising framework for collaboratively training machine learning models across decentralized genomic datasets without direct data sharing. While this approach preserves data locality, it remains susceptible to sophisticated inference attacks that can compromise individual privacy. In this study, we simulate a federated learning setup using synthetic genomic data and assess its vulnerability to three key attack vectors: Membership Inference Attack (MIA), Gradient-Based Membership Inference Attack, and Label Inference Attack (LIA). Our experiments reveal that Gradient-Based MIA achieves the highest effectiveness, with a precision of 0.79 and F1-score of 0.87, underscoring the risk posed by gradient exposure in federated updates. Additionally, we visualize comparative attack performance through radar plots and quantify model leakage across clients. The findings emphasize the inadequacy of naïve FL setups in safeguarding genomic privacy and motivate the development of more robust privacy-preserving mechanisms tailored to the unique sensitivity of genomic data.

This directly matches the user's proposed contribution.

papers.title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
papers.abstract:
Deep neural networks are susceptible to various inference attacks as they remember information about their training data. We design white-box inference attacks to perform a comprehensive privacy analysis of deep learning models. We measure the privacy leakage through parameters of fully trained models as well as the parameter updates of models during training. We design inference algorithms for both centralized and federated learning, with respect to passive and active inference attackers, and assuming different adversary prior knowledge.
  We evaluate our novel white-box membership inference attacks against deep learning algorithms to trace their training data records. We show that a straightforward extension of the known black-box attacks to the white-box setting (through analyzing the outputs of activation functions) is ineffective. We therefore design new algorithms tailored to the white-box setting by exploiting the privacy vulnerabilities of the stochastic gradient descent algorithm, which is the algorithm used to train deep neural networks. We investigate the reasons why deep learning models may leak information about their training data. We then show that even well-generalized models are significantly susceptible to white-box membership inference attacks, by analyzing state-of-the-art pre-trained and publicly available models for the CIFAR dataset. We also show how adversarial participants, in the federated learning setting, can successfully run active membership inference attacks against other participants, even when the global model achieves high prediction accuracies.

papers.title: A Framework for Evaluating Gradient Leakage Attacks in Federated Learning
papers.abstract: Federated learning (FL) is an emerging distributed machine learning framework for collaborative model training with a network of clients (edge devices). FL offers default client privacy by allowing clients to keep their sensitive data on local devices and to only share local training parameter updates with the federated server. However, recent studies have shown that even sharing local parameter updates from a client to the federated server may be susceptible to gradient leakage attacks and intrude the client privacy regarding its training data. In this paper, we present a principled framework for evaluating and comparing different forms of client privacy leakage attacks. We first provide formal and experimental analysis to show how adversaries can reconstruct the private local training data by simply analyzing the shared parameter update from local training (e.g., local gradient or weight update vector). We then analyze how different hyperparameter configurations in federated learning and different settings of the attack algorithm may impact on both attack effectiveness and attack cost. Our framework also measures, evaluates, and analyzes the effectiveness of client privacy leakage attacks under different gradient compression ratios when using communication efficient FL protocols. Our experiments also include some preliminary mitigation strategies to highlight the importance of providing a systematic attack evaluation framework towards an in-depth understanding of the various forms of client privacy leakage threats in federated learning and developing theoretical foundations for attack mitigation.

This directly aligns with the user's idea of a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits gradient update history to reconstruct whether a specific sample was used in local training. papers.title: Concealing Sensitive Samples against Gradient Leakage in Federated Learning
papers.abstract: Federated Learning (FL) is a distributed learning paradigm that enhances users privacy by eliminating the need for clients to share raw, private data with the server. Despite the success, recent studies expose the vulnerability of FL to model inversion attacks, where adversaries reconstruct users private data via eavesdropping on the shared gradient information. We hypothesize that a key factor in the success of such attacks is the low entanglement among gradients per data within the batch during stochastic optimization. This creates a vulnerability that an adversary can exploit to reconstruct the sensitive data. Building upon this insight, we present a simple, yet effective defense strategy that obfuscates the gradients of the sensitive data with concealed samples. To achieve this, we propose synthesizing concealed samples to mimic the sensitive data at the gradient level while ensuring their visual dissimilarity from the actual sensitive data. Compared to the previous art, our empirical evaluations suggest that the proposed technique provides the strongest protection while simultaneously maintaining the FL performance.
papers.reason: Problem: The user's idea focuses on a membership inference attack that reconstructs whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch by exploiting gradient update history. The paper, however, addresses the problem of defending against 'model inversion attacks' where adversaries 'reconstruct users' private data via eavesdropping on the shared gradient information'. While both relate to gradient leakage in federated learning, the paper's problem is about reconstructing the data itself, whereas the user's idea is specifically about inferring membership of a sample, which is a distinct type of privacy attack. | Method: The user's research idea proposes a membership inference attack, which is an adversarial technique. In contrast, the evaluated paper proposes a defense strategy called 'Concealing Sensitive Samples (DCS2)' against model inversion attacks. The paper's method involves synthesizing concealed samples to obfuscate gradients, which is a fundamentally different approach from performing a membership inference attack. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on membership inference attacks in federated learning that exploit gradient information. The paper explicitly discusses 'model inversion attacks, where adversaries reconstruct users private data via eavesdropping on the shared gradient information' and aims to 'reconstruct whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch' by leveraging 'gradient update history'. This directly aligns with the user's idea of 'reconstructing whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch' by exploiting 'the gradient update history from a compromised participant'. | Contribution: The user's research idea proposes a membership inference attack, which aims to reconstruct whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch. In contrast, the evaluated paper focuses on a defense strategy against model inversion attacks, which aim to reconstruct the private data itself from shared gradients. These are distinct problems with different objectives, even though both operate within the federated learning context and involve gradient information.

existing_examples.name: Membership Inference Attacks in Federated Learning
existing_examples.description: Numerous research papers have explored membership inference attacks (MIA) specifically in the context of federated learning. These attacks often leverage gradient information or model updates to infer membership.

existing_examples.name: Gradient-based MIAs
existing_examples.description: Many MIAs, both in centralized and federated settings, exploit the information leakage from gradients. The idea of reconstructing training data or inferring membership from gradient updates is a well-established research area.

existing_examples.name: Specific research papers (e.g., 'Membership Inference Attacks Against Federated Learning' by Nasr et al., 'Exploiting Federated Learning for Membership Inference' by Shokri et al. (though primarily for centralized, it laid groundwork))
existing_examples.description: Several seminal and follow-up works directly address membership inference in federated learning by analyzing client updates/gradients. The concept of a 'compromised participant' (e.g., an adversarial server or another client) is also common in threat models.

reality_check.assessment: The idea describes a well-known and actively researched area: membership inference attacks (MIA) specifically targeting federated learning (FL). The core mechanism of exploiting gradient update history from a compromised participant to infer sample membership is a standard approach in this field. Many existing works have demonstrated and analyzed such attacks.
novelty_aspects: Specific novel techniques for gradient reconstruction or analysis (e.g., new loss functions, optimization methods, or statistical tests for inference)
Application to a very specific, previously unstudied federated learning architecture or data type
Quantifying the attack's effectiveness under novel privacy-preserving mechanisms (e.g., a new differential privacy variant or secure aggregation scheme)
reality_check.recommendation: While the general concept is not novel, there is still room for research in developing more sophisticated attack methodologies, evaluating existing attacks against new FL paradigms or defense mechanisms, or providing stronger theoretical guarantees for such attacks. Focus on a very specific, underexplored angle or a significant improvement over existing attack techniques.

matched_sections.paper_title: An Efficient Gradient-Based Inference Attack for Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: Gradient-Based Membership Inference Attack
matched_sections.text_snippet: As previously mentioned, [13] observed that the gradients of the loss function differ significantly between training and non-training samples. Their attack involved computing gradients with respect to all parameters across all model layers. However, this is computationally demanding, and their approach assumes access to part of the private data. In contrast, we propose a novel attack that reduces computational demands while maintaining effective inference. It is based on the idea that the final model layers store enough training-related information.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea explicitly mentions 'exploits the gradient update history', which aligns with the paper's focus on gradient-based attacks and the observation that gradients differ between training and non-training samples. The paper's method specifically focuses on 'last-layer gradients', which is a refinement of the general 'gradient update history'.

matched_sections.text_snippet: Federated Learning (FL) offers a promising framework for collaboratively training machine learning models across decentralized genomic datasets without direct data sharing. While this approach preserves data locality, it remains susceptible to sophisticated inference attacks that can compromise individual privacy.
matched_sections.reason: The paper focuses on federated learning applied to genomic data, which is the exact domain specified in the user's idea.

matched_sections.paper_title: Securing Genomic Data Against Inference Attacks in Federated Learning Environments
matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: This paper focuses on understanding and quantifying the privacy risks associated with federated learning in the genomic context. Specifically, we examine how inference attacks can be leveraged to extract private information from model updates in a synthetic genomic FL setup.
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly states its focus on federated learning in the genomic context and the privacy risks from inference attacks, aligning perfectly with the user's domain.

matched_sections.paper_title: Securing Genomic Data Against Inference Attacks in Federated Learning Environments
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: Our experiments reveal that Gradient-Based MIA achieves the highest effectiveness, with a precision of 0.79 and F1-score of 0.87, underscoring the risk posed by gradient exposure in federated updates.
matched_sections.reason: The paper's core contribution is demonstrating the effectiveness of Gradient-Based MIA in federated learning by exploiting gradient exposure, which is the exact contribution proposed by the user.

matched_sections.paper_title: Securing Genomic Data Against Inference Attacks in Federated Learning Environments
matched_sections.heading: DISCUSSION
matched_sections.text_snippet: Gradient-Based MIA achieved even higher success with an F1-score exceeding 0.86, largely due to the availability of gradient information during local training. This highlights how exposing even intermediate computations (like gradients) in federated protocols can leak membership status. The high recall suggests that nearly all training data points could be reliably inferred with the tuned gradient threshold [31] [32].
matched_sections.reason: The paper's discussion reinforces the finding that Gradient-Based MIA effectively exploits gradient information in federated protocols to infer membership, directly matching the user's proposed contribution.

layer1_results.paper_title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
layer1_results.reason: Problem: The paper directly addresses the user's research idea. It explicitly states, 'We design white-box inference attacks to perform a comprehensive privacy analysis of deep learning models. We measure the privacy leakage through parameters of fully trained models as well as the parameter updates of models during training. We design inference algorithms for both centralized and federated learning, with respect to passive and active inference attackers, and assuming different adversary prior knowledge.' This aligns perfectly with the user's idea of a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits gradient update history. | Method: The paper proposes a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits gradient updates, which is very similar to the user's idea. Specifically, the paper states, 'We design new algorithms tailored to the white-box setting by exploiting the privacy vulnerabilities of the stochastic gradient descent algorithm' and 'We also show how adversarial participants, in the federated learning setting, can successfully run active membership inference attacks against other participants'. The paper also details 'The Gradient Ascent Attacker' which 'adversarially manipulates the learning process to improve the membership inference accuracy' by 'updating their data features towards ascending the gradients of the global model (in case of the global attack) or the local model (in the case of a local attack)', directly aligning with the user's concept of exploiting gradient update history. | Domain: The user's research idea focuses on a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning. The paper directly addresses this by designing 'white-box membership inference attacks against deep learning algorithms' in 'federated learning settings' and explicitly states 'we show how adversarial participants, in the federated learning setting, can successfully run active membership inference attacks against other participants'. The paper also details exploiting 'the gradient update history from a compromised participant' by discussing 'The Passive Global Attacker' and 'The Gradient Ascent Attacker' in federated learning, which aligns perfectly with the user's idea of reconstructing whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch. | Contribution: The paper directly addresses the user's research idea. It explicitly states, "We also show how adversarial participants, in the federated learning setting, can successfully run active membership inference attacks against other participants, even when the global model achieves high prediction accuracies." Furthermore, it details the exploitation of gradient updates, stating, "The active attacker can exploit the SGD algorithm to run the active attack... This directly matches the user's proposal of a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits gradient update history to reconstruct sample usage.

sentence_level.sentence: A membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits the gradient update history from a compromised participant, reconstructing whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch.

matched_sections.paper_title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: We evaluate our novel white-box membership inference attacks against deep learning algorithms to trace their training data records. We also show how adversarial participants, in the federated learning setting, can successfully run active membership inference attacks against other participants, even when the global model achieves high prediction accuracies.
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly states its focus on white-box membership inference attacks, including active attacks by adversarial participants in federated learning, which directly aligns with the user's problem of a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning.

matched_sections.paper_title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: In a membership inference attack, however, the attacker's objective is to infer if a particular individual data record was included in the training dataset [4], [5], [6]. This is a decisional problem, and its accuracy directly demonstrates the leakage of the model about its training data.
matched_sections.reason: The paper defines membership inference attack as inferring whether a specific data record was included in the training dataset, which is precisely the objective stated in the user's idea: 'reconstructing whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch'.

matched_sections.text_snippet: The Gradient Ascent Attacker: In this scenario, the attacker adversarially manipulates the learning process to improve the membership inference accuracy. matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: We therefore design new algorithms tailored to the white-box setting by exploiting the privacy vulnerabilities of the stochastic gradient descent algorithm, which is the algorithm used to train deep neural networks.

matched_sections.paper_title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: Attack Mode: Passive vs. Active Inference Attack
matched_sections.text_snippet: The active attacker can exploit the SGD algorithm to run the active attack. The insight we use to design our attack is that the SGD algorithm forcefully decreases the gradient of the loss on the training data, with the hope that this generalizes to the test data as well. The amount of the changes depends on the contribution of a data point in the loss.
matched_sections.reason: The paper details an active attack exploiting the SGD algorithm and changes in gradients based on data point contribution, which aligns with the user's method of exploiting 'gradient update history'.

matched_sections.paper_title: Comprehensive Privacy Analysis of Deep Learning: Passive and Active White-box Inference Attacks against Centralized and Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: Attack Mode: Passive vs. Active Inference Attack
matched_sections.text_snippet: Let x be a data record, which is targeted by the adversary to determine its membership. Let us assume the adversary is one of the participants. The attacker runs a gradient ascent on x , and updates its local model parameters in the direction of increasing the loss on x . This can simply be done by adding the gradient to the parameters, where γ is the adversarial update rate. The adversary then uploads the adversarially computed parameters to the central server, who will aggregate them with the parameter updates from other participants.
matched_sections.reason: The paper describes an active attack where a participant performs gradient ascent on a target record and uploads adversarially computed parameters, directly exploiting gradient updates in a federated setting, similar to the user's 'exploits the gradient update history from a compromised participant'.

The active attack is described in Section II-C. We evaluate the attack accuracy on predicting the membership of 100 randomly sampled member instances, from the target model, and 100 non-member instances.

matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: Additionally, in a variety of real-world settings, the parameters of deep learning algorithms are visible to the adversaries, e.g., in a federated learning setting where multiple data holders collaborate to train a global model by sharing their parameter updates with each other through an aggregator.

matched_sections.heading: Inference Target: Stand-alone vs. Federated Learning
matched_sections.text_snippet: There are two major types of training algorithms for deep learning, depending on whether the training data is available all in one place (i.e., stand-alone centralized training), or it is distributed among multiple parties who do not trust each other (i.e., federated learning) [8].

matched_sections.heading: CONCLUSIONS

layer1_results.paper_title: A Framework for Evaluating Gradient Leakage Attacks in Federated Learning
layer1_results.reason: Problem: The user's research idea describes a 'membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits the gradient update history from a compromised participant, reconstructing whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch.' The paper directly addresses this by stating, 'adversaries can reconstruct the private local training data by simply analyzing the shared parameter update from local training (e.g., local gradient or weight update vector)' and 'The client privacy leakage attack is a gradient-based feature reconstruction attack, in which the attacker can design a gradient-based reconstruction learning algorithm that will take the gradient update at round t, say ∇wk(t), to be shared by the client, to reconstruct the private data used in the local training computation.' This is essentially the same problem and framing. | Method: The paper's proposed 'Client Privacy Leakage (CPL) Attack' is a gradient-based reconstruction attack that iteratively reconstructs private training data from shared gradient updates, which is very similar to the user's idea of a membership inference attack exploiting gradient update history to reconstruct sample usage. The paper explicitly compares its CPL-patterned attack with existing gradient leakage attacks like 'deep gradient attack [45]' and 'gradient inverting attack [10]', indicating that its method is an advancement within a very similar methodological family rather than a fundamentally new approach. The core mechanism of reconstructing data from gradients is shared. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on membership inference attacks in federated learning. The paper explicitly discusses 'gradient leakage attacks' and 'client privacy leakage attacks' in federated learning, aiming to reconstruct private local training data by analyzing shared parameter updates (e.g., local gradients), which directly aligns with the user's idea of exploiting gradient update history to reconstruct whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch. | Contribution: The paper's core contribution is a framework for evaluating gradient leakage attacks in federated learning, which includes reconstructing private training data from shared gradient updates. This directly aligns with the user's idea of a membership inference attack tailored to federated learning that exploits gradient update history to reconstruct whether a specific sample was used in local training. The paper explicitly states, "adversaries can reconstruct the private local training data by simply analyzing the shared parameter update from local training (e.g., local gradient or weight update vector)" and details 'Algorithm 1 Gradient-based Reconstruction Attack' which reconstructs training data from gradients.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Framework for Evaluating Gradient Leakage Attacks in Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: However, recent studies have shown that even sharing local parameter updates from a client to the federated server may be susceptible to gradient leakage attacks and intrude the client privacy regarding its training data.
matched_sections.reason: The paper identifies the problem of gradient leakage attacks in federated learning that intrude client privacy regarding training data, which aligns with the user's idea of a membership inference attack exploiting gradient update history to reconstruct whether a sample was used.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Framework for Evaluating Gradient Leakage Attacks in Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Several recent research efforts have shown that the default privacy in FL is insufficient for protecting the underlaying training data from privacy leakage attacks by gradient-based reconstruction [10,45,43]. By intercepting the local gradient update shared by a client to the FL server before performing federated averaging [28,25,17], the adversary can reconstruct the local training data with high reconstruction accuracy, and hence intrudes the client privacy and deceives the FL system by sneaking into client confidential training data illegally and silently, making the FL system vulnerable to client privacy leakage attacks (see Section 2.2 on Threat Model for detail).
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly states that adversaries can reconstruct local training data by intercepting local gradient updates, which is the core problem the user's idea aims to address through a membership inference attack.

matched_sections.paper_title: A Framework for Evaluating Gradient Leakage Attacks in Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: The Client Privacy Leakage (CPL) Attack: An Overview
matched_sections.text_snippet: The client privacy leakage attack is a gradient-based feature reconstruction attack, in which the attacker can design a gradient-based reconstruction learning algorithm that will take the gradient update at round t , say ∇ w k ( t ), to be shared by the client, to reconstruct the private data used in the local training computation.
matched_sections.reason: The paper describes a gradient-based reconstruction attack that uses gradient updates to reconstruct private training data, which is a very similar mechanism to the user's proposed membership inference attack exploiting gradient update history to reconstruct sample usage.

matched_sections.reason: The paper mentions 'membership attacks' as a type of privacy exploit, which is the specific type of attack the user proposes. While the paper's description of membership attacks focuses on prediction differences, the user's idea tailors it to federated learning by exploiting gradient history, indicating a methodological adaptation rather than a completely new method.

layer1_results.paper_title: Concealing Sensitive Samples against Gradient Leakage in Federated Learning
layer1_results.reason: Problem: The user's idea focuses on a membership inference attack that reconstructs whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch by exploiting gradient update history. The paper, however, addresses the problem of defending against 'model inversion attacks' where adversaries 'reconstruct users' private data via eavesdropping on the shared gradient information'. While both relate to gradient leakage in federated learning, the paper's problem is about reconstructing the data itself, whereas the user's idea is specifically about inferring membership of a sample, which is a distinct type of privacy attack. | Method: The user's research idea proposes a membership inference attack, which is an adversarial technique. In contrast, the evaluated paper proposes a defense strategy called 'Concealing Sensitive Samples (DCS2)' against model inversion attacks. The paper's method involves synthesizing concealed samples to obfuscate gradients, which is a fundamentally different approach from performing a membership inference attack. | Domain: Both the user's research idea and the paper focus on membership inference attacks in federated learning that exploit gradient information. The paper explicitly discusses 'model inversion attacks, where adversaries reconstruct users private data via eavesdropping on the shared gradient information' and aims to 'reconstruct whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch' by leveraging 'gradient update history'. This directly aligns with the user's idea of 'reconstructing whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch' by exploiting 'the gradient update history from a compromised participant'. | Contribution: The user's research idea proposes a membership inference attack, which aims to reconstruct whether a specific sample was used in a victim client's local training batch. In contrast, the evaluated paper focuses on a defense strategy against model inversion attacks, which aim to reconstruct the private data itself from shared gradients. These are distinct problems with different objectives, even though both operate within the federated learning context and involve gradient information.

matched_sections.text_snippet: Recent model inversion attacks (Zhu, Liu, and Han 2019; Geiping et al. 2020; Balunovi´ c et al. 2022; Fowl et al. 2022; Li et al. 2022) have shown that the users' private data can be reconstructed from the gradients shared during the learning process.

matched_sections.paper_title: Concealing Sensitive Samples against Gradient Leakage in Federated Learning
matched_sections.heading: Privacy Leakage
matched_sections.text_snippet: Optimization-based attacks aim to reconstruct data by minimizing the distance between the gradient of the input and that of the reconstruction. In contrast, model modification attacks utilize specific parameters with the goal of amplifying the leakage of individual data points (Boenisch et al. 2021) within the mini-batch or allowing portions of the gradient to contain information exclusive to a subset of data points (Fowl et al. 2022).

linked_sections.paper_title: An Efficient Gradient-Based Inference Attack for Federated Learning
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: In this work, we present a new gradient-based membership inference attack for federated learning scenarios that exploits the temporal evolution of last-layer gradients across multiple federated rounds.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper explicitly state the problem of a membership inference attack in federated learning.

linked_sections.paper_title: Securing Genomic Data Against Inference Attacks in Federated Learning Environments
linked_sections.heading: EXPERIMENTAL SETUP
linked_sections.text_snippet: · Gradient-Based Membership Inference Attack: -Objective: Infer membership status using the norm of per-sample gradients. -Rationale: Gradients for training samples typically have smaller magnitudes due to optimization convergence, while non-members exhibit larger gradients. -Implementation: Gradient norms were computed per sample. A fixed threshold (e.g., 0.5) was used to classify samples as members or non-members.
linked_sections.reason: The paper details the implementation of a Gradient-Based Membership Inference Attack that exploits per-sample gradient norms to determine membership, which is precisely the method described by the user.

linked_sections.paper_title: An Efficient Gradient-Based Inference Attack for Federated Learning
linked_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
linked_sections.text_snippet: Federated Learning is a machine learning setting that reduces direct data exposure, improving the privacy guarantees of machine learning models. Yet, the exchange of model updates between the participants and the aggregator can still leak sensitive information.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper are explicitly set within the domain of 'federated learning'.

linked_sections.reason: The paper's core contribution is demonstrating the effectiveness of Gradient-Based MIA in federated learning by exploiting gradient exposure, which is the exact contribution proposed by the user.

To improve originality, consider a novel application domain or a fundamentally different approach to mitigating gradient leakage.
